cl_2023_pc_10_q_1.instructions:
Direction (1-4): Study the following information and answer the questions that follow:

Of course, we don’t currently have the means of achieving human immortality, nor is it clear that we ever will. But two hypothetical options have so far attracted the most interest and attention: rejuvenation technology, and mind uploading.

Like a futuristic fountain of youth, rejuvenation promises to remove and reverse the damage of ageing at the cellular level. Gerontologists such as Aubrey de Grey argue that growing old is a disease that we can circumvent by having our cells replaced or repaired at regular intervals. Practically speaking, this might mean that every few years, you would visit a rejuvenation clinic. Doctors would not only remove infected, cancerous or otherwise unhealthy cells, but also induce healthy ones to regenerate more effectively and remove accumulated waste products. This deep makeover would ‘turn back the clock’ on your body, leaving you physiologically younger than your actual age. You would, however, remain just as vulnerable to death from acute trauma – that is, from injury and poisoning, whether accidental or not – as you were before.

Rejuvenation seems like a fairly low-risk solution, since it essentially extends and improves your body’s inherent ability to take care of itself. But if you truly wanted eternal life in a biological body, it would have to be an extremely secure life indeed. You’d need to avoid any risk of physical harm to have your one shot at eternity, making you among the most anxious people in history.

The other option would be mind uploading, in which your brain is digitally scanned and copied onto a computer. This method presupposes that consciousness is akin to software running on some kind of organic hard-disk – that what makes you is the sum total of the information stored in the brain’s operations, and therefore it should be possible to migrate the self onto a different physical substrate or platform. This remains a highly controversial stance. However, let’s leave aside for now the question of where ‘you’ really reside, and play with the idea that it might be possible to replicate the brain in digital form one day.

Unlike rejuvenation, mind uploading could actually offer something tantalisingly close to true immortality. Just as we currently back up files on external drives and cloud storage, your uploaded mind could be copied innumerable times and backed up in secure locations, making it extremely unlikely that any natural or man-made disaster could destroy all of your copies.

Despite this advantage, mind uploading presents some difficult ethical issues. Some philosophers, such as David Chalmers, think there is a possibility that your upload would appear functionally identical to your old self without having any conscious experience of the world. You’d be more of a zombie than a person, let alone you. Others, such as Daniel Dennett, have argued that this would not be a problem. Since you are reducible to the processes and content of your brain, a functionally identical copy of it – no matter the substrate on which it runs – could not possibly yield anything other than you.
cl_2023_pc_10_q_1.question_text: Which of the following, if true, would invalidate the claim that the mind uploading presents some difficult ethical issues?
options: There is a scientific consensus on the nature and origin of consciousness and its relation to the brain.
There is a legal framework that regulates and oversees the mind uploading process.
There is a moral agreement that values the autonomy of conscious experience of self and the world.
There is a philosophical acceptance that embraces the diversity and plurality of uploaded minds.
cl_2023_pc_10_q_1.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 1
Your Answer : 1
This option is correct. One of the main ethical issues of mind uploading is the possibility of zombie uploads, which are functionally identical to the original person but lack conscious experience. This would raise questions about the identity and moral status of the uploaded mind. If there is a scientific consensus on how to measure and verify consciousness and its relation to the brain, then this issue would be resolved. We would be able to tell whether an uploaded mind is a zombie or not, and whether it deserves moral rights or not.

Wrong Options:
2) There is a legal framework that regulates and oversees the mind uploading process.
This option is incorrect. A legal framework that regulates and oversees the mind uploading process may not reflect the moral values or preferences of all individuals or groups. It may also be subject to change or challenge over time. Moreover, a legal framework may not address all the ethical issues that may arise from mind uploading, such as privacy, data security, social and economic implications, moral responsibility, etc. Therefore, a legal framework alone would not invalidate the claim that mind uploading presents some difficult ethical issues.
3) There is a moral agreement that values the autonomy of conscious experience of self and the world.
This option is incorrect. A moral agreement that values the autonomy of conscious experience of self and the world may not apply to all cases or scenarios of mind uploading, especially if there are different types of consciousness or selfhood. For example, some uploaded minds may prefer to merge with other minds or create multiple copies of themselves, which may challenge the notion of autonomy or individuality. Moreover, a moral agreement may not address all the ethical issues that may arise from mind uploading, such as privacy, data security, social and economic implications, moral responsibility, etc. Therefore, a moral agreement alone would not invalidate the claim that mind uploading presents some difficult ethical issues.
4) There is a philosophical acceptance that embraces the diversity and plurality of uploaded minds.
This option is incorrect. A philosophical acceptance that embraces the diversity and plurality of uploaded minds may not address the practical challenges or conflicts that may arise from having multiple copies or versions of oneself or others. For example, some uploaded minds may compete or conflict with each other or with biological humans over resources, rights, or interests. Moreover, a philosophical acceptance may not address all the ethical issues that may arise from mind uploading, such as privacy, data security, social and economic implications, moral responsibility, etc. Therefore, a philosophical acceptance alone would not invalidate the claim that mind uploading presents some difficult ethical issues.

1) Your uploaded mind could interact and merge with other uploaded minds, creating new forms of sociality and intimacy that transcend the limitations of biology.

3) Mind uploading is based on the assumption that consciousness resides in brain operations.
This is incorrect because this statement provides a foundational concept for mind uploading and its relation to consciousness, contributing to the depth of the discussion.

cl_2023_pc_10_q_4.question_text: As per the passage, which one of the following best sums up the overall purpose of rejuvenation?
options: To extend human lifespan by modifying and improving the functions of cells and organs.
To alter the appearance of a person by removing and replacing harmful and diseased cells.
To improve human function by avoiding and undoing the cellular harm caused by ageing
To preserve the physiological youth by manipulating and stimulating the activity of cells
cl_2023_pc_10_q_4.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 3
Your Answer : 3
This is the correct answer because it captures the main idea of rejuvenation as stated in the passage. The passage says that rejuvenation promises to remove and reverse the damage of ageing at the cellular level, which implies that it aims to improve human function by avoiding and undoing the cellular harm caused by ageing.

Wrong Options:
1) To extend human lifespan by modifying and improving the functions of cells and organs. This is incorrect because it changes the wording and meaning of the passage. The passage does not say that rejuvenation modifies or improves the functions of cells and organs, but rather that it removes and reverses the damage of ageing.
2) To alter the appearance of a person by removing and replacing harmful and diseased cells. This is incorrect because it narrows down the scope and purpose of rejuvenation. The passage does not say that rejuvenation only affects the appearance of a person, but rather that it affects the whole body at the cellular level. Moreover, the passage does not say that rejuvenation only removes and replaces harmful and diseased cells, but also that it induces healthy ones to regenerate more effectively and removes accumulated waste products.
4) To preserve the physiological youth by manipulating and stimulating the activity of cells. This is incorrect because. Manipulating or stimulating is too broad. Rejuvenation specifically refers to inducing healthy cells to regenerate and remove waste.

cl_2023_pc_10_q_5.instructions:
Direction (5-8): Study the following information and answer the questions that follow:

Imagine, then, what would happen if each time a new study was done, the researchers had to rely on different, small datasets created using different data-collection protocols. That scenario may sound absurd, yet it describes the actual situation facing researchers trying to understand our species’ evolutionary history though the anatomical study of ancient fossils. Often, the most interesting remains are physically accessible to only a small number of scientists. Everyone else is then forced to rely on published descriptions or second-hand impressions. Yet many recent finds raise huge questions about human evolution that are impossible to answer properly without broad access to the raw data: the fossils themselves.

A more rigorous and desirable approach to evaluating hypotheses in anthropology is to compile large datasets, which allows study of whole populations. Nonetheless, because the currency of a study’s scientific power has been the number of observations, scientists have tended to publish as little of the raw values as possible in order to avoid helping a peer-competitor build a dataset and get the scoop on interesting results. The culture of ‘data holding’ has promoted frustrating inefficiencies. Many specimens in anthropology collections are pockmarked where dozens of researchers have set their callipers to take the same measurements again and again. Another consequence of a lack of access to raw datasets is that it is exceedingly difficult to determine whether apparent differences in anatomical patterns among living species and fossils from one study to the next are due to observer error, measurement protocols, the use of different samples, or genuine evolutionary features.

The digital format makes it possible to distribute raw data about specimen anatomy around the world in an instant, with the potential to throw the datasets wide open, allowing direct assessments of repeatability and compilation into ‘super-datasets’ from which everyone works. Such a systemic change could transform the entire study of human origins and evolutionary biology writ large.

To aid that transformation, my colleagues and I at Duke University have created a website called MorphoSource that archives 3D data from microCT scanners, surface scanners and photogrammetry, and more. Researchers can then access those files from anywhere they have an internet connection. It is, in essence, a global repository of virtual fossils and comparative anatomical data. The intellectual rewards of such open sharing for the advancement of science are obvious, but if we want the researchers and museums to open their collections for digital distribution, there needs to be direct incentives for those holding the collections, as well. By its mere existence, MorphoSource creates a pressure for greater data-sharing. Previously, there was no system that could systematically accommodate large numbers of detailed digital datasets on museum specimens. Now that MorphoSource is live, editors and grant reviewers are beginning to forcefully suggest, and sometimes require, that researchers reposit their raw datasets for access there.

Many institutions and individuals intuitively sense that open access is too soft for their hard-won data, or they may have contracted with stakeholders that take this view. MorphoSource therefore allows researchers and institutions merely to ‘advertise’ the existence of datasets, which then require an active request for access.
cl_2023_pc_10_q_5.question_text: Which of the following statements, if true, would least support the idea that MorphoSource is useful to researchers?
options: It provides a platform for public access to data for researchers across the world.
It creates a pressure for greater data-sharing, leading many institutions to advertise their datasets.
Editors are beginning to forcefully suggest that researchers reposit their raw datasets for access.
It allows researchers to modify the 3D data according to their needs and preferences.
cl_2023_pc_10_q_5.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 4
Your Answer : 4
This statement does not support the idea that MorphoSource is useful to researchers because it does not show how MorphoSource facilitates the access, sharing or preservation of 3D data from fossils and anatomy. It also implies that the original data may be altered or corrupted by the researchers, which could undermine the reliability and integrity of the data. MorphoSource should provide researchers with accurate and authentic 3D data that reflect the true state and condition of the specimens, not allow them to modify the data according to their needs and preferences.

Wrong Options:
1) It provides a platform for public education and outreach on the topics of fossils and anatomy.
This statement supports the idea that MorphoSource is useful to researchers because it shows how MorphoSource can help them disseminate their findings and knowledge to a wider audience, such as students, teachers, journalists, policymakers and the general public. By providing a platform for public education and outreach, MorphoSource can also increase the visibility and impact of the researchers' work, as well as foster public interest and appreciation for the topics of fossils and anatomy. The passage talks of sharing data with researchers. To call it public education would be farfetched.
2) It creates a pressure for greater data-sharing, leading many institutions to advertise their datasets.
This statement supports the idea that MorphoSource is useful to researchers because it shows how MorphoSource can encourage them to share their data more openly and transparently, which can enhance the quality and reproducibility of their research. By creating a pressure for greater data-sharing, MorphoSource can also facilitate collaboration and communication among researchers from different institutions and disciplines, as well as promote the reuse and reanalysis of existing data for new purposes.
3) Editors are beginning to forcefully suggest that researchers reposit their raw datasets for access.
This statement supports the idea that MorphoSource is useful to researchers because it shows how MorphoSource can help them meet the expectations and requirements of the scientific community and the publishing industry. By having editors forcefully suggest that researchers reposit their raw datasets for access, MorphoSource can also ensure that the researchers adhere to the ethical and professional standards of data management and stewardship, as well as increase the credibility and accountability of their research.

cl_2023_pc_10_q_6.question_text: In the context of the passage, none of the following is implied, EXCEPT?
options: Researchers who post their unprocessed data benefit their peers more than those who do not.
In anthropology, large datasets are not typically preferred to small ones.
Data holding is a common practice among anthropologists who study fossils.
The culture of data holding has no benefits for the scientific community.
cl_2023_pc_10_q_6.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 1
Your Answer : 4
This is the correct answer because it can be inferred from the passage that posting the raw values would help other researchers to build large datasets and test hypotheses on whole populations. The passage states that scientists have tended to publish as little of the raw values as possible in order to avoid helping a peer-competitor get the scoop on interesting results.

Wrong Options:
2) In anthropology, large datasets are not typically preferred to small ones.
This is incorrect because it states an incorrect fact. In reference to, “the currency of a study’s scientific power has been the number of observations” it can be inferred that large datasets are preferable
3) Data holding is a common practice among anthropologists who study fossils.
This is incorrect because it is too extreme. The passage states that data holding is a culture that has promoted frustrating inefficiencies, but it does not imply that it is a common practice among all anthropologists who study fossils. There may be some researchers who share their raw data or use different methods to measure fossils.
4) The culture of data holding has no benefits for the scientific community.
This is incorrect because it is too extreme and negative. The passage states that data holding has led to wasteful repetition of measurements and difficulty in distinguishing between real and spurious anatomical differences, but it does not imply that it has no benefits at all for the scientific community. There may be some advantages of data holding, such as protecting one's intellectual property or ensuring quality control.

A sentence of LP Harley’s novel about love, class and innocence reads: ‘The past is a foreign country: they do things differently there.’ But do they, really? And, if so, just how differently, in fact, did people do things in that peculiar foreign country that this adage invites us to think of as ‘the past’?

This is an elementary question. It is especially complicated for historians of antiquity, far removed as this passage of human history is from present experience, contemporary sensibilities, and the whole taken-for-granted way in which most of us live our familiar, day-to-day lives. To really go there – to take seriously the proposition that people did things differently in the past, and to pursue the implications wherever they might lead – requires a leap of historical imagination that must, in practice, go a little bit further than what can be ‘proven’ by any one piece of evidence. All historians acknowledge differences between past and present, of course, and sometimes quite significant differences, in terms of technology, economic organisation, social structure and so on, but most end up treating the people of the past more or less as versions of ourselves, caught up in different contexts, to be sure, but essentially interchangeable with us. Not many scholars, that is, are quite prepared to dive into the deep alienness of the past.

The dangers of getting things wrong by assuming a casual familiarity with antiquity are revealed when we consider a topic of enduring fascination to ancient historians: the widespread phenomenon of public giving and civic benefaction in the Greco-Roman world. At a glance, this public giving looks a lot like the contemporary practice of high-profile charitable donations, the establishment of major foundations, the patronage of the arts, and so on. And there are indeed some similarities between ancient magnates and their modern counterparts when it comes to such public displays of generosity. Through a closer look at the expenditures of the Roman elite in particular, though, we can discern the outlines of a historically distinctive modality of public giving. It was French historian Paul Veyne who provided that closer look in the 800 pages of his masterwork, Le pain et le cirque

The whole practice of public expenditure and civic benefaction in the ancient Mediterranean world has come to be known as ‘euergetism’, a neologism derived from ancient Greek and meaning, literally, ‘the doing of good deeds’. When Veyne probed euergetism in depth, however, comparing it not to modern charitable donations but rather to the ceremony of the potlatch among the Indigenous peoples of the Pacific Northwest (a typically startling interpretive move), he exploded many of our assumptions about what the practice signified. Aimed at a collectivity of citizens and not at the individual poor, civic benefactions were not like Christian charity. Local elites, towering above the masses of their communities, hardly needed to advertise their wealth as a way to reinforce their social standing. This rules out any simple equivalence between euergetism and ‘conspicuous consumption’.

'Conspicuous consuming' was a secular practise, whereas euergetism was a sort of Christian almsgiving.

Contrary to “Euergetism” that took place in private, "conspicuous consumption" is a public clear public activity
cl_2023_pc_10_q_9.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 3
This is the correct answer because the passage states that local elites "hardly needed to advertise their wealth as a way to reinforce their social standing". This implies that euergetism was not a means of displaying their wealth or status, unlike conspicuous consumption.

Wrong Options:
1) 'Conspicuous consumption' was directed at a collective of citizens, whereas euergetism was focused at specific impoverished people.
This is incorrect because the passage states that euergetism was "aimed at a collectivity of citizens and not at the individual poor". This contradicts the option.
2) 'Conspicuous consuming' was a secular practise, whereas euergetism was a sort of Christian almsgiving.
This is incorrect because the passage states that euergetism was "not like Christian charity". This implies that euergetism was not a religious practice, unlike almsgiving.
4) Contrary to “Euergetism” that took place in private, "conspicuous consumption" is a public clear public activity.
This is incorrect because the passage does not mention anything about the privacy or publicity of euergetism or conspicuous consumption. This option is irrelevant to the passage.

This is not a wrong inference because it is not contradicted by the passage, which focuses on the differences in sensibilities and ways of doing things, rather than on technology or economic organisation. The author does not deny that there are material differences between past and present, but rather emphasizes that there are also other differences that are more subtle and profound.

One area where the comparative approach has had a substantial impact is in our understanding of the evolution of the vocal tract. Lieberman et al (1969) note an unusual feature of human vocal tract that appears to set it apart from the primate norm. Our larynx is positioned rather low in the throat. This is puzzling because it means that we must coordinate breathing and swallowing carefully to avoid choking. If our larynx was higher, as it is in other mammals and in human infants, then it could be projected into the nasal cavity allowing us to breathe and swallow at the same time.

Lieberman (1969) suggests that this apparently counter-functional trait in humans is actually the result of an adaptation to communication. The descent of the larynx over our evolutionary history radically changed the shape of the vocal tract from one which essentially had the acoustic properties of a straight tube to one where that tube has a bend in the middle (where the oral cavity meets the pharynx). What this does is increase the diversity of vowel sounds that we can produce, which in turn increases the informational carrying capacity of the vocal channel.

More recently, Fitch & Reby (2001) have shown that there are other possible adaptive advantages to a descended larynx by looking carefully at the comparative data. It turns out that other mammals actually do lower their larynxes during vocalisation, and indeed some species have a permanently lowered larynx in the male of the species. These animals certainly do not have complex vocal communication that requires the enhanced carrying capacity of a vocal tract with a bend in it, so what is going on?

What lowering the larynx does in addition to creating a bend in the vocal tract is increase the total length of the tract. This changes the acoustic properties of vocalisations in such a way as to increase the perceived size of the animal making the sound. Fitch (2000) suggests that it is this perceived size enhancement that is the driving force behind the descent of the larynx in species without complex vocalisations. Animals, particularly males, that appear to be large may be more successful in competition for mates, and in avoiding predation. If this is the case, then might it not also be a factor in the evolution of the human vocal tract? Certainly, even in humans there is some sexual dimorphism, with the male larynx undergoing a second descent around puberty.

The communication systems of primates and human language are disparate in structure and intricacy. How did language transmute from a rudimentary to a sophisticated system? Some people inquire “what good is half a language?” like they inquire “what good is half an eye?”. They surmise that something as elaborate as an eye or a language could not have evolved incrementally. But there are intermediate forms of eyes that are advantageous for different animals. The study of protolanguage, a conjectural primordial language from which extant languages are derived, aims to demonstrate that there are also intermediate forms of language that are propitious for communication.

This option is not supported by the passage, which does not suggest that the eye and language have a common origin. The eye and language are different systems that evolved independently in different domains.
3) Both are illustrations of adaptability and natural selection.

Late by 12 minutes
Early by 15 minutes
Late by 10 minutes
cl_2023_pc_10_q_35.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 1


Rashi starts from home at 8:00 AM and reaches station F at 8:04 AM.
Time taken by a train that leaves station A and travels via G-H-C-D to reach station F
= 9 + 2 + 9 + 2 + 9 + 3 + 16.5 + 3 + 9 = 62.5 minutes
So the train that leaves A at 7:00 AM reaches station F after 62:30 minutes at 8:02:30 AM and it leaves after 2 minutes at 8:04:30 AM.
Rashi will be able to board the train as she is at the station at 8:04 AM.
Time taken by the train that leaves station F at 8:04:30 AM and travels via E-B to reach station A = 9 + 2 + 9 + 3 + 16.5 = 39.5 minutes
So Rashi will reach her office at 8:04:30 + 0:39:30 + 0:03:00 = 8:47 AM.
Hence, Rashi is early by 13 minutes.

cl_2023_pc_10_q_59.question_text: A group of men, all of equal efficiency, were hired to work for Rs. 20,000. The group work started with one man starting the work, joined by another on the second day, then by one more on the third day and …so on till the last man joined on the last day when the work was completed. If every man worked at half of his normal efficiency, then the work was completed in exactly 15 days. Find the maximum amount (in Rs.) earned by any man in the group.
cl_2023_pc_10_q_59.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 2500
Here each man works at the same rate finally.
Therefore, only the number of days worked by any person is relevant for finding the wage share of that person.
So maximum possible share is of the person who started i.e.,
15/(15 + 14 +....+ 2 + 1) × 20,000 = (15 × 2)/(15 × 16) × 20000 = Rs. 2,500.

cl_2023_pc_10_q_66.question_text: The number of integral pairs (a, b) satisfying the equation a2 = b2 + 1294 is:
cl_2023_pc_10_q_66.solution:
Solution:
Correct Answer : 4
We have a2 – b2 = 1294
Since a, and b are integers, a2 – b2 is an even number.
So both a2 and b2 must be even numbers or odd numbers. This means both a and b must be even or odd. In either case, both a + b and a – b must be even numbers and hence a2 – b2 must be divisible by 4. But 1294 is not divisible by 4.
Hence, no integral pairs (a, b) can satisfy the given equation.
